S:
Patient is a well-known female with symptomatic partial epilepsy secondary to static encephalopathy, cerebral palsy, and shunted hydrocephalus related to prematurity, and factor V Leiden deficiency. Last seen in neurology clinic 11/16/2007. Trileptal dose was maximized to 2 mL BID without sedation. On Friday, she had a 25-minute seizure with eye deviation, unresponsiveness, and posturing, treated with Diastat. Trileptal dose increased to 3 mL BID yesterday. Since shunt revision on 12/18/2007, she has been sleepier than normal. Six days ago, she became more lethargic with vomiting and low-grade fevers. Vomiting stopped after hospitalization and receiving medication in the ER. She remains lethargic, does not wake spontaneously, but is appropriate and interactive when awake. Eating well, but overall PO intake is diminished. Less "feisty" than usual. Seizure-free since admission.

O:
Pertinent labs: CRP < 0.3, CMP normal, CBC within normal limits. CSF cultures negative so far. CSF analysis (per Dr. Limon's note): WBC 2, RBC 1, glucose 55, protein 64. No imaging studies available in the computer.

A:
Patient with history of epilepsy, cerebral palsy, and shunted hydrocephalus presenting with lethargy, recent seizure, and possible post-shunt revision complications. Seizure occurred despite Trileptal dose increase. Lethargy and decreased PO intake are concerning. Labs and CSF analysis are largely unremarkable, ruling out acute infection or significant metabolic derangement based on available data.

P:
Continue current management. Monitor neurological status, PO intake, and vital signs. Await further diagnostic information, including review of imaging studies if available from Kaweah Delta Hospital. Consider further investigation into the cause of lethargy if it persists or worsens. Continue Trileptal at 3 mL BID.

S: 80-year-old African-American male with a history of asbestos exposure, admitted with worsening shortness of breath and constipation. He complains of cough and blood mixed with sputum production, but denies fever. Past medical history includes BPH, PUD, atrial fibrillation, CAD, aortic valve replacement (St. Jude mechanical valve #23 in 1991), ICD implantation, PVD, CABG (1991, 1998), and CHF (EF 40%).

O: General: Elderly male, awake, alert, oriented, no acute distress. Vitals: BP 136/80, P 70, RR 20, T 99.3, SpO2 96% on 2L NC. HEENT: Peripheral cyanosis. Neck: Supple. Lungs: Bibasilar crackles, decreased breath sounds left base. Cardiovascular: Regular rate/rhythm, murmur, metallic click. Abdomen: Soft, benign. Extremities: 1+ cyanosis, no clubbing, no edema. Labs: WBC 6.9, Hgb 10.6, Hct 31.2, Plt 160,000, CK 266, PTT 37, PT 34, INR 3.7, Na 141, K 4.2, Cl 111, CO2 23, BUN 18, Cr 1.7, Glu 91, Ca 8.6, TP 6.1, Alb 3.3, T Bili 1.

A: 80-year-old male with history of asbestos exposure, CHF, and mechanical aortic valve presenting with hemoptysis, worsening shortness of breath, and constipation. Physical exam findings include peripheral cyanosis, bibasilar crackles, and decreased breath sounds in the left base. Labs show anemia (Hgb 10.6), elevated creatinine (1.7), and supratherapeutic INR (3.7).

P: Further evaluation and management of hemoptysis, shortness of breath, and constipation. Monitor vital signs and oxygen saturation. Continue current medications, adjust Coumadin dose based on INR. Monitor renal function and electrolytes. Further diagnostic workup as indicated.

P:
Continue routine well-child care. Reassure mother regarding spitting up. Educate on signs of concerning reflux (projectile vomiting, poor weight gain, respiratory symptoms). Follow up as scheduled.

S:
46-year-old white male with Down’s syndrome presents for followup of hypothyroidism and onychomycosis. He completed six weeks of Lamisil without problems and is due for an ALT check. He was found to be hypothyroid with elevated TSH in April and started on Levothroid 0.1 mg daily, with TSH recheck today. Mother reports he takes medications with encouragement. Other medications include eyedrops for cornea.

O:
Weight 149 lbs (up 2 lbs). BP 120/80. Pulse 80 and regular. Neck supple without adenopathy, no thyromegaly or nodules. Cardiac regular rate and rhythm without murmurs. Skin examination shows toenails are still thickened and yellowed with no change.

A:
1. Down’s syndrome.
2. Onychomycosis.
3. Hypothyroidism.

P:
1. Recheck ALT and TSH today and call results.
2. Lamisil 250 mg #30 one p.o. daily with one refill; continue for eight more weeks if ALT is normal. Reviewed symptoms of liver dysfunction.
3. Continue Levothroid 0.1 mg daily unless dosage needs adjustment based on TSH.
label: h200-nvl/medgemma-27b-text-it/soap_en

S:
The patient is a 53-year-old right-handed gentleman presenting for evaluation of diplopia that began in January 2009. Initially, the diplopia resolved when closing either eye and lasted about an hour, but recurred the next day, worsening over the next two weeks. He was evaluated by a primary care physician, ophthalmologist, and in the emergency department where an MRI was performed. The MRI showed a questionable 3 mm aneurysm of the medial left supraclinoid internal carotid artery, but was otherwise negative, including MRV and MRI brain with/without contrast. The diplopia improved over the next month but remains constant, with the perceived distance between the two images decreasing. He does not drive or work due to the diplopia. Over the past month, he has developed right supraorbital pain, feeling like pain under his right lid. He denies dysphagia, dysarthria, weakness, numbness, tingling, or other neurological symptoms.

O:
Neurology consultation on February 2, 2009, noted MRI findings of a questionable 3 mm aneurysm of the medial left supraclinoid internal carotid artery. MRV was negative. MRI of the brain with and without contrast was negative.

A:
Diplopia, constant, with recent onset of right supraorbital pain. History of questionable 3 mm aneurysm of the medial left supraclinoid internal carotid artery on MRI.

P:
Further evaluation for diplopia and right supraorbital pain. Follow up as needed.
label: h200-nvl/medgemma-27b-text-it/soap_en
